title: Right Relationship
subtitle: Generosity, Renunciation, and the Perfections
epigraph: If the whole of your life you said thank you, that would be enough. — Meister Eckhart
paragraphs: I thought I'd continue that theme of relationship. Normally at Christmas I do the paramis, the ten perfections. The perfections were—it translates to the word parama, which means the other shore. It's what takes you to the other shore. They were a later collection of what the commentaries thought was important about the Buddha's teachings.

Just to put it in, I'll speak them and then you can see what they are. There's dana, giving; there's morality; there's renunciation; there's wisdom, which is a mental virtue; energy, patience, truthfulness, resolution, loving-kindness, and equanimity. Those are the ten that were chosen by the wise ones out of the Buddhist teachings. And as you know, the Mahayana only have six, so that makes us much more superior.

Just to talk about relationships. Remember that we were trying to say last night that there's a part of the Buddha's teachings about insights, about actually understanding who and what we are and all that, and how we create suffering. But the other part is how it expresses itself in the world through our relationships.

Occasionally when I watch these videos on TED—have you come across TED? It's a website that has lots of talks by experts and such. Some are very good; I like them. But these people who are into positive psychology, you come across this word connections. That's their way of expressing relationship, which to me is really just about reducing it to some sort of communication in a way. Because academics will always try to find another word on the presumption that they're coming up with something remarkably new. It's quite difficult to use ordinary words like relationship.

The fundamental relationship that the Buddhas talk about, and which is throughout the East of course, is ahimsa, no harm. This was variously translated. The Jains, as you know, thought that when you did harm, even if you didn't mean it, you were going to get your comeuppance. So they basically avoided stepping on ants and all that. They'd come back and bite you in the bum or something. And you see them brushing their way before they take a step—a very great sensitivity to the amount of harm we can do just because either we're not aware of it or we don't want to be aware of it. It's just ignorance, is it?

But from the Buddha's point of view, that wasn't really the problem. His problem was always to do with how we create suffering for ourselves. Therefore it was an internal thing. And he brought it down to this whole business of intention, empowering an intention. When we empower intention, remember, you create a habit. An action—the usual progression: an intention, an action, a habit, a personality, and a destiny. That's the trajectory.

Our personalities are just these combinations of habits. And it's really recognising what a habit's doing; if it's doing any harm, then to undermine it, to not go with those particular intentions. But our inner intentional life, of course, expresses itself outwardly in obvious ways in what we do, in more subtle ways in body language. And this immediately creates a connection, to use that wonderful word. You're establishing a relationship.

And the relationships are telling us what we're giving out in the world, even when we're not actually fully aware of it. People with passive aggression, for instance—where the person's obviously manifesting aggression, but when you say they're angry, they say, 'Not me, I never get angry.' It's that inability to see ourselves as we are, even in relationship.

But when something happens in a relationship, it's mirroring something back to us. Of course, you've always got to take into consideration that the other person's doing the same. That's another thing we say, isn't it—that when two people meet, there are six people there. There's me as I see myself, there's me as you see me, and there's me as I really am. And the same applies to you. In this web of delusion, it's very difficult to work out how or why things go wrong.

But in terms of the Buddha's teachings, at the root of this problem lies this acquisitiveness, this desire, and deeper than that, this sense of thirst, of lack. Other writers use that word, lack. And in order to undermine that, we practise certain things that cut deep into that business of finding safety, finding happiness in acquisition. That's the process of renunciation.

Again, I'm sure you all know the distinction that's often made between renunciation and mortification. Mortification was based on some idea that the soul was held down by the heaviness of the body. You get it in Christian understanding too, don't you? 'If the eye offends you, pluck it out.' People often believe it's the body at fault. Sex is at fault. Our sexual powers are what's evil, not the way we use them. That's the devil drawing us down into the pleasures of life.

One idea at that time, of course, was that as you undermined the pleasures of the body, the soul released itself. And you often get that sort of self-mortification in other religions too. The Buddha saw that as just more suffering. But he didn't give up the idea of renunciation. It shifted—the definition shifted—and it shifted to giving something up to see where the attachment was. It's as simple as that.

Our fast, depending on what came up for you in the fast, would show you at times your attachment to certain patterns of behaviour. Now that would be a sort of negative use of renunciation. In other words, you're positively giving something up in order to see where your attachment is.

For instance, we might disguise our loneliness—everybody feels lonely at some time or other—by distracting ourselves: watching TV, listening to radio, always having some input, so we're never actually in contact with the inner person. And then when you go into a silence and there's nothing there and you're not distracting the mind, suddenly you're on your own. And in that aloneness, there's a feeling of being abandoned, unloved. Nobody loves me. How could they? I'm horrible. It's getting in touch with that nasty feeling of loneliness and unlovedness.

But of course we would do it in order to get in touch with it, to feel it, to actually begin to explore that feeling and find out where its basis was, where it's coming from. And we also know that in that exploration, that malevolent energy that we've created through attachment—we have defined love as how much another person can fulfil our needs. That's what we understand love to be. So when there's nobody there to fulfil our needs, then of course I'm completely unloved. I mean, no Christmas present. I don't get any more Christmas presents.

When we cut deep into that—and when we say cut deep into it, we're not trying to understand it intellectually; that takes you off somewhere else. Cutting deep means going right into the pain of loneliness. Take that as an example. Then, of course, we find that it begins to transform. The transformation happens quite naturally. It can happen very suddenly. Suddenly you find it's gone and you're perfectly happy with yourself and you've discovered solitude. Other times it fades out, but there's a murky sort of stuff there, not going anywhere. But even so, some of that energy is in the process of transforming, of moving to the upper. In fact, I'm trying to use this new word, sublimating. It just—there's no in-between state; it goes from one state to the other.

That's the negative, to use that word, use of renunciation. We're trying to find out where we're stuck, where we're holding on to things, trying to let go of things. But any time we behave from the heart of love, there must be some form of renunciation.

The Buddha points this out in dana. That's one of his main teachings, dana, which is generosity. Whenever he's talking to lay people, he always starts with dana. And he points out that anybody can be generous, even a thief. After they've stolen your money, they can give it to somebody else who's in need. Or Robin Hood. So generosity comes even before sila, which means these five basic rules of no harm that we chant every morning. It's even before that. You can be a really ugly, terrible person, but still be kind to your dog and give it biscuits when it's hungry. So that's the easiest one to generate.

One of the things that excites—and this is the little matrix that spins around—are these three things. What makes generosity very easy, at least to practise, is developing gratitude. I remember once reading Meister Eckhart, the thirteenth-century mystic, who said that if the whole of your life you said thank you, that would be enough. That's how important he felt this attitude of gratitude was. Thankfulness.

So this little virtuous circle of gratitude—when you think about just what we've received from society. I remember somebody grumbling to me once that she didn't have any children, and she didn't know why she paid taxes so that other children could be educated. She found that absolutely obnoxious. When you begin to realise that actually a lot of our education has been given to us by the generous taxation of other people—and it's just things like that, things that we take for granted because of our social system. Doctors, the NHS—there's all sorts of things which we presume now; we don't see them as something which is gifted to us. Not that we don't pay our taxes, but... So, gratitude enlivens this idea of generosity.

And as soon as we give something of time or of wealth, we must be giving it away from ourselves. We must be giving something up. In a sense, there are various degrees of generosity. At Christmas, people swap gifts. That's what kings do—when they meet, they swap gifts. It's just a friendship thing, a social nicety, you might say. But it has a purpose. Yet it's difficult to call it generosity, because if your lump of cheese didn't turn up, you'd be very upset, since you've gone and bought them another different type of cheese. So it's that sort of swapping of things, which is just a social nicety.

But when it comes to real generosity, you know that you are being generous when, in a sense, what you're giving actually is pushing the boundaries of what you want to give—without, of course, harming yourself. One doesn't want to give all one's wealth away and then end up on the streets.

There's a lovely story of St. Francis. One of his monks or friars, taking this business of complete poverty as a standard, met somebody who needed clothes. So he gave him his robe and came back naked. And so Francis had to make a rule that you can give everything away apart from your robe. There's a certain sort of—we have to use our common sense.
But it's when you give of time—give of time—that you know that when that boundary is being pushed, you are truly beginning to renounce something.

So I was just talking to my aunt there, and she belongs to that old school—she's in her mid-eighties or something. She was telling me that when Uncle John got sick, he was very old, she never went to bed for two years. She sat in a chair. One day he got up; he had a little stroke, and at night he'd be disoriented. So one night she caught him trying to get out of the window, and this really freaked her. So what she did was put her armchair next to the window and sat there for two years, never went to bed. She would amuse herself by listening to the radio and knitting and doing all sorts of stuff, just for fear that he'd be sleepwalking and do something stupid. And it ruined her sleep pattern, which she's never particularly regained. And I'm just thinking, well, I wonder if I would do that. I'd probably just chain them to the bed, put a manacle on their foot.

So this generosity, of course, you can expand into really all sorts of areas. One of them—and it's funny, it's not actually in the Ten Perfections; I don't know why—is, I think, presumed in the word love. Because when we chant the loving-kindness discourse, you can see that the Buddha is giving it a very wide meaning of just a loving relationship, which includes both compassion and joy.

So when something comes up which demands us to help somebody who is in need, then the generosity manifests as compassion. It's the same when somebody's rejoicing over something—to rejoice with them, to be happy for them, is also an act of generosity. Even more so if you feel absolutely miserable and the world's gone completely wrong for you, but you're able to put that aside and rejoice with the person who's being happy. That's also an act of generosity.
So generosity, in the widest sense of the term, is giving of oneself. Emptying, giving oneself, emptying oneself towards another.

It's very beautiful how the Buddha talks about his relationship to the people he teaches. He says, whatever a teacher can do for his pupils, I have done for you, out of anukampan. Anukampan means to move towards somebody. The word that most will know is karuna, which is compassion. But the word he uses, anukampan, is to move towards somebody. So when you see somebody who is in need, you move towards them.

And it was one of the things in his big doubt, wasn't it? The great doubt. Remember that in the great doubt, when Mara arose and said, who the hell are you to try and become liberated from all suffering? You've been around everybody, you know everybody, and nobody's made it. And here you are... Where's this ego thing coming from, this pride?

And it's when he touches the earth and the earth goddess arises. She defends him by saying, because of his past lives, perfecting the virtue of generosity. So what does this mean? It means that he saw that what he was doing wasn't just for himself; it was for all beings. And that gave him the courage to withstand the doubt, the inner doubt. What was he doing this for? This is ridiculous.

And that perfection of generosity was basically—if you want an interpretation of that story—that was the backbone of his effort. It wasn't just a matter of him trying to liberate himself from suffering, but also remembering—he probably remembered his own family and his son and all that sort of stuff. And then from there, all his friends and people like that. And it must have occurred to him that if he can crack this problem, then he's cracked it for everybody.

And if we were to talk about happinesses, you can talk about three levels. One is the ordinary sensual happiness, which is fleeting and all that, but it's a happiness—physical happiness. It can also be intellectual, artistic, even the joys of the practice. When some peace arises very occasionally, that's a bit of transient joy, and to be enjoyed.

And then there's that type of joy where you get lost in it. I think Aristotle called it eudaimonia. It's when you get lost, when you lose the self in something—like a DVD. And then you come out of it and suddenly you're there again. During that moment, you've been perfectly happy. You've not been self-aware, but you have been perfectly happy. And then you come out of it and it's gone. And it's catching that gone-ness that brings up this idea of not getting attached to something that you've become so involved in for happiness.

But what makes people really, really deeply happy—these surveys and whatnot show this—has nothing to do really with transient emotions, because they arise and pass away, and always sense your pleasure. It is meaningfulness. And when that meaningfulness goes, you have despair. That's why I say people who lose meaningfulness in their lives are really on the point of despair.

There are these soldiers who commit suicide after war and whatnot, for whatever reason, psychologically. But seemingly, they all commit suicide because at some point they despair. They despair. And I had a woman phone me up just the other day in a dreadful state, just at the point of despair—despair, just when you give up hope. And of course, that's the sign, isn't it, on Dante's hell: abandon all hope, ye who enter here.

So this idea of generosity has this very deep way of connecting us. And it's only then that our lives seem to take on a much deeper meaning. And in that meaning, that connectedness, that very deep relationship—that is really love at its deepest level, isn't it? And when that's extended from human beings to animals and nature and the world and the cosmos and all that, then you suddenly find yourself in this lovely place of being interconnected. That's what it means, isn't it? And it comes about through generosity. It comes about through giving, compassion. It doesn't come about because you've thought about it and you've written an essay on interconnectedness. No. It actually comes about just because you've made that effort to give of yourself into that huge matrix.

And you can even see some of the other ones. Sīla—that's the dark side. That's why we say you shouldn't kill and all that. And take what's not freely given and all those things. Negative things of not to do that, not to do that. And I think you'll find that every time you are behaving unethically, even in the smallest, simplest, silly way that creates little qualms in the mind, there'll be something against one of those five.

The whole rule of the Vinaya, apart from those that are specific to little rules and regulations, are all around these five basic rules. They're fundamental, aren't they, to our society, fundamental to our relationship. So in correcting all the negative stuff that we do on those sides—anger, the usual stuff, being callous, little sharp words that we might say to people—by correcting all that, that is also to be seen as a generous act, because you're giving up this inner desire to poke somebody in the eye, which can be very pleasurable. That's sīla.

Renunciation. Wisdom. Patience. Patience is another one. Now the Buddha talks about it in a more—what would you call it? I'm trying to think of another word apart from negative. It's not negative. It's just the other side of the more positive language. The Buddha talks about it as the greatest form of ascetic practice.

So what does he mean by ascetic practice? He means bearing with suffering. So the self-mortifier starves themselves, sits next to fires for the heat, stands in the river Ganges for hours on end so the karma is washed away. And there's all sorts of wonderful ascetic practices. There was even a Christian saint—I can't remember his name now—but he sat on a pillar. He was up there all his life. And that was it. He lived on the top of this pillar. So these are things that probably came from the East. And you'll still see in India ascetics who've kept their arm up in the air and it's withered. I've seen that myself. They have reasons for doing these things.

So what he means by ascetic practice is to bear with, patient forbearance. And of course, it begins inward, doesn't it? Because all this stuff comes up and we've just got to patiently bear with these emotional states that come up within ourselves. But they're the same states that we see in other people. There's nothing that separates us apart from the stories. Everybody has fear. Everybody has depression, all that. But the stories are all different. They're all different dramas. But fundamentally, we've all got these same experiences of life.

And bearing with somebody who's annoying us is an act of generosity, isn't it? You're taking it on. Of course, you don't want to be abused by them, because that's another thing. When you talk about love and whatnot, people get confused that you have to give, give, give, even though you're being throttled. Give of your neck. There's a limit—as soon as the person's hurting you, then of course a different moral comes in where your love has lost its connection with you personally.

And he gives that lovely example, doesn't he? Somebody came along with a machete. He doesn't use that word, but he came along with a sword and chopped you from limb to limb. If ever you were even to be angry, you're no disciple of mine. I once said that in another talk. Somebody said, well, the Buddha had a great sense of humour. But I think he meant it.

So this idea of bearing with people, patient forbearance—it's a perfection. And it undermines all the other stuff as well, especially the whole thing about victimisation, being the victim and how people get caught up in that. "Why me?" It moves into self-pity. And what happens is they're unable to forgive. So they're locked into a really dreadful state. They just can't get out of that because they have been hurt. Nobody's denying that. But there's that self-pity. "Why me?" And the hatred. And so long as there's self-pity, you can't forgive. There's a block.

And it's only when you begin to put yourself in the shoes of the other person who's done the harm and recognise that they also are going to have a lot of suffering because of the harm they've done. And how do we know that? By investigating the harm that we've done and recognising that actually, internally, I'm not looking at the amount of suffering in me for the harm I've done to somebody else. And the greater the harm, the more difficult it is to forgive oneself. So that whole victim thing can be a real lock. And patient forbearance is part of that process of forgiveness.

There's, of course, energy, compassion. The Buddha is very tough on energy. The word comes up right there in the discourse on establishing right awareness—tapo, ātāpī, ātāpī—which comes from this word tapo, which comes up in the Maṅgala Sutta there. And it's the energy that you need to really complete a task, whatever it is, to complete the task, to finish it, whatever you undertake.

Often people come on a retreat, it gets a bit tough. They made a resolution to stay the week, it gets a bit tough, and towards the end they give up. Now, what they're doing is undermining their ability to perfect their commitments. So any time it gets a bit difficult, there's always this little escape.

That doesn't mean to say... On retreats, I'm sure you've heard me say to commit yourself to the course, save an extraordinary circumstance or emergency. So it's not as though it's an absolute thing. If the place caught fire, you're not supposed to sit there and say, "I will not leave this place." So there's also common sense.

But whenever we say we're going to do something and we don't do it, we're actually harming ourselves. So, for instance, you might say to a friend, "Yeah, I'll come and decorate." And then you phone to tell them you're not feeling so well today. And it's like you're undermining this ability to make resolutions, to make determinations.

And if you think again of the Buddha's own practice, the moment of great determination—to sit under that tree, either to crack this problem or to die. And that's where he got to. It was almost, you might say, a point of despair. He had this little insight. He had this insight coming from childhood to actually investigate suffering. But he could see no other way. And if this didn't work, well, that was it. So he was prepared to sit there until he died with it. We call that the great determination. So that's this business of energy.

The energy is needed, but resolution is the other perfection. But you need that energy. When you suffer from dullness and lethargy, remember that is also an energy. So you don't look at it negatively. Don't think to yourself, "Well, this is something dragging me down." It is dragging you down, but it's an actual force. It's a mental energy. It can also be physical. And if we just allow it to manifest, it also transforms, it also sublimates into its opposite, which is good energy. Sometimes it'll flip and become restlessness, but when it's actually moving towards its opposite, it's moving into this lovely flow of energy when our meditation is very easy for us.

Truthfulness here is something quite specific. It's really keeping your promise rather than not telling great big porkies. That's part of it, but it's actually when you say you'll do something that you actually do it. So you can see that that melds into the whole thing of resolution and such.

And the other one is wisdom, of course. I've not put them in the same order here. Wisdom is a mental—call it that—a mental virtue. So it's really understanding things. And really it all begins there, doesn't it? If we've misunderstood something, then things go awry. So we always have to come back to this, the Buddha's constant encouragement to reflect wisely—wise reflection, yoniso manasikāra.

So at the end of a sitting, just get in the habit of picking up even one thing from your sitting, letting it come to mind. And at the end of a day, just look back for a moment, pick up one or two things. We have to be very careful not to always pick up the negative stuff. We're very good at that. We have to also pick up the positive stuff—positive things that have happened to us today, what we've done today.

And that's another thing about joy. You've got to actually excite joy within yourself. You've got to remember times when you were joyful and understand how that joy came about. This builds up a well of joy. We tend to be a bit negative usually, don't we? We tend to remember all the horrible things, because they hurt. The pleasurable things that don't hurt, they're all right. But by doing that, we get a lopsided view of our lives sometimes. It's all miserable and horrible and all that. We forget that we've just had a good meal.

So these are our pāramitā. The final one is equanimity. And it's arguable—definitely Nyanaponika Thera, who wrote that very good book, The Heart of Buddhist Meditation. It still is really a classic book. But he also wrote other essays, and in one he argues quite strongly that the perfection of equanimity is really the perfection because it balances everything.

Although equanimity can have its own dark energy, its own dark side of indifference, it does have this ability to stop these things overriding. So that love doesn't become attachment. So seeing where the attachment is. How do you know? How do you know when you're attached? Well, there are many ways, aren't there? Especially when you're getting irritated with somebody because they're not doing what you want them to do. That's an obvious case of attachment. Or when they disappoint you, when you're disappointed in somebody. Expectation, stuff like that.

Compassion slips into pity, doesn't it? Pity for somebody. And if you think about how you feel when somebody pities you, then you know how horrible it is for somebody who's being pitied by you. So equanimity stops that. And again, it's this business of being open to what the other person is asking for, not getting into that do-gooder thing.

Joy slips into excitement. So if you take any virtue, you'll see it has a subtle enemy. Courage slips into foolhardiness. But if you think of any virtue, it'll always have its slightly subtle negative side that it can slip into. We can talk about generosity. It slips into over-generosity. It goes beyond what you're actually capable of doing, and you undermine yourself by doing that.

So that's a quick run-through of these pāramitā. It does say here, these qualities were developed and brought to maturity by the Bodhisattva in his past existences, in this way practising them, illustrating the many birth stories of the Jātaka. However, it's only the verses that are regarded as canonical. That's of the stories around the Buddha—where he's reborn as a hare and so on. It's all Aesop's fables, really.
So we can end this just by me reading this lovely little paragraph which expresses what the Bodhisattva, or the person who wants to develop these ten perfections, aspires to:

As the great beings—see Mahāsattva, so that's synonymous really for the Bodhisattva—destined for Buddhahood are concerned with the welfare of living beings, not tolerating the suffering of beings, wishing long duration to the higher states of happiness of beings, and being impartial and just to all beings, therefore they give alms to all beings so that they may be happy, without investigating whether or not they are worthy. By avoiding to do them any harm, they observe morality. And in order to bring morality to perfection, they train themselves in renunciation. In order to understand clearly what is beneficial and injurious to beings, they purify their wisdom. And for the sake of the welfare and happiness of others, they constantly exert their energy. Though having become heroes through utmost energy, they are nevertheless full of forbearance towards the manifold failings of beings. Once they have promised to give or to do something, they do not break their promise. That's the truthfulness. With unshakable resolution, they work for the welfare of beings. And with unshakable kindness, they are helpful to all. And by reason of their equanimity, they do not expect anything in return.

It's nice, isn't it? I remember once this causing a bit of a discussion as to whether you should give without investigating whether they are worthy or not. This is commentary, remember, so we can feel free to question that one.
So I hope my words have been of some assistance. And that you will soon perfect the perfections and enter into that wonderful place, Nibbāna, sooner rather than later.
